course_info.title: Anatomy and Physiology
course_info.meeting_schedule: Tuesday, Thursday - 3 hours lecture + 2 hours lab per week

learning_objectives: Classify the four primary tissue types and their subtypes
Describe the structure and functions of the integumentary system
Understand wound healing and common skin pathologies

lecture_notes.introduction: Anatomy and Physiology builds on your biology foundation with detailed study of body structures and their functions. This deeper understanding is essential for interpreting clinical documentation and diagnostic data.
key_concepts: Four tissue types: epithelial (covering/lining), connective (support/binding), muscle (movement), nervous (communication)
Epithelial subtypes: simple/stratified, squamous/cuboidal/columnar
Integumentary system: epidermis, dermis, hypodermis, accessory structures
Skin functions: protection, thermoregulation, sensation, vitamin D synthesis
Wound healing phases: hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, remodeling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Histology lab: identify tissue types from prepared microscopy slides
setup_requirements: Microscopy slides or virtual histology software

review.value: Lines body surfaces and cavities, classified by layers (simple/stratified) and cell shape (squamous/cuboidal/columnar)
Four overlapping phases: hemostasis (clotting), inflammation (immune response), proliferation (new tissue growth), remodeling (scar maturation)

class_schedule.topic: Skeletal System: Bone Structure and Joints
learning_objectives: Describe bone tissue composition and the process of ossification
Identify the major bones of the axial and appendicular skeleton
Classify joints by structure and function

lecture_notes.introduction: The skeletal system provides framework, protection, and movement. Understanding bone anatomy is essential for interpreting orthopedic records, fracture classifications, and musculoskeletal imaging.
key_concepts: Bone composition: compact (cortical) bone and spongy (cancellous) bone
Ossification: intramembranous (flat bones) and endochondral (long bones)
Axial skeleton: skull, vertebral column (C7, T12, L5, sacrum, coccyx), thoracic cage
Appendicular skeleton: upper and lower limbs, pectoral and pelvic girdles
Joint classification: fibrous (immovable), cartilaginous (slightly movable), synovial (freely movable)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Skeletal anatomy lab: identify bones and landmarks on a skeletal model, connecting to radiographic anatomy
setup_requirements: Skeleton model or virtual anatomy software

review.value: 7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, sacrum (5 fused), coccyx (4 fused) - spinal level identification is critical in clinical documentation
Freely movable joint with articular cartilage, joint cavity with synovial fluid, and joint capsule - most common joint type, site of arthritis

learning_objectives: Explain the sliding filament mechanism of muscle contraction
Identify major muscle groups and their actions

lecture_notes.introduction: Muscles produce movement, maintain posture, and generate heat. Understanding muscle physiology connects to clinical concepts like EMG testing, neuromuscular diseases, and rehabilitation.
key_concepts: Muscle types: skeletal (voluntary), cardiac (involuntary), smooth (involuntary)
Sliding filament theory: actin, myosin, cross-bridge cycling, ATP role
Neuromuscular junction: motor neuron, acetylcholine, motor end plate
Major muscle groups: prime movers, antagonists, synergists, fixators
Clinical connections: EMG, muscle biopsy, myasthenia gravis, muscular dystrophy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Muscle physiology lab: EMG measurement of forearm muscles during grip strength testing

review.value: Muscle contracts when myosin heads bind actin, pull thin filaments toward center of sarcomere, release, and repeat - powered by ATP hydrolysis
Synapse between motor neuron and muscle fiber where acetylcholine release triggers muscle contraction - target of diseases like myasthenia gravis

learning_objectives: Describe the organization of the central and peripheral nervous systems
Explain how neurons generate and transmit electrical signals
Understand the structure and functions of major brain regions

lecture_notes.introduction: The nervous system is the body's control and communication center. Its complexity is reflected in the extensive neurological documentation found in clinical records.
key_concepts: CNS: brain and spinal cord; PNS: cranial and spinal nerves, autonomic nervous system
Neuron structure: cell body, dendrites, axon, myelin sheath, synaptic terminals
Action potential: resting potential, depolarization, repolarization, propagation
Brain regions: cerebrum (lobes), cerebellum, brainstem (medulla, pons, midbrain)
Autonomic nervous system: sympathetic (fight-or-flight) vs. parasympathetic (rest-and-digest)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Neurological assessment lab: reflex testing, cranial nerve examination, sensory testing

review.value: Rapid reversal of membrane polarity traveling along the axon: Na+ channels open (depolarization), then K+ channels open (repolarization) - the basis of nerve signal transmission
Sympathetic: prepares body for stress (increases HR, dilates pupils). Parasympathetic: promotes rest and digestion (decreases HR, stimulates digestion)

learning_objectives: Identify the major endocrine glands and their hormones
Explain hormonal regulation through feedback mechanisms
Understand diabetes mellitus pathophysiology in detail

lecture_notes.introduction: The endocrine system regulates metabolism, growth, and reproduction through hormones. Diabetes is the most common endocrine disorder and one of the most data-intensive conditions in health informatics.
key_concepts: Endocrine glands: pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal, pancreas, gonads
Hormone types: steroid, peptide, amino acid-derived and their mechanisms of action
Negative feedback: the dominant regulatory mechanism (e.g., thyroid axis: TRH-TSH-T3/T4)
Diabetes Type 1: autoimmune destruction of beta cells, insulin deficiency
Diabetes Type 2: insulin resistance, progressive beta cell failure, metabolic syndrome
lecture_notes.demonstration: Glucose regulation simulation: trace insulin and glucagon responses to meals and fasting, then model what happens when the system fails (Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes)

review.value: Regulatory mechanism where the output of a process inhibits its own production - e.g., high T3/T4 levels inhibit TSH release from the pituitary
Insulin resistance in target tissues (muscle, liver, fat) combined with progressive beta cell dysfunction, resulting in hyperglycemia and metabolic complications

learning_objectives: Describe the anatomy of the kidney and nephron
Explain the three processes of urine formation
Understand fluid and electrolyte balance and common renal diseases

lecture_notes.introduction: The kidneys filter blood, regulate fluid balance, and maintain electrolyte homeostasis. Renal function tests are among the most frequently ordered labs, making kidney physiology highly relevant to health informatics.
key_concepts: Kidney anatomy: cortex, medulla, renal pelvis, nephron as functional unit
Urine formation: glomerular filtration, tubular reabsorption, tubular secretion
Hormonal regulation: ADH (water reabsorption), aldosterone (Na+ reabsorption), ANP
Renal function tests: GFR, BUN, creatinine, urinalysis
Renal diseases: acute kidney injury, chronic kidney disease (stages 1-5), dialysis, transplant
lecture_notes.demonstration: Urinalysis lab: interpret simulated urinalysis results for normal and abnormal scenarios (UTI, diabetes, kidney disease)

review.value: Glomerular Filtration Rate: the best overall measure of kidney function (~125 mL/min normal), used to stage CKD (Stage 1: >90, Stage 5: <15)
Filters blood (glomerulus), selectively reabsorbs needed substances (tubules), and secretes wastes to produce urine - each kidney contains ~1 million nephrons

questions.question: Explain the sliding filament mechanism of skeletal muscle contraction, starting from a nerve impulse arriving at the neuromuscular junction. Include the roles of calcium, ATP, actin, and myosin.
questions.answer: A nerve impulse triggers acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction, depolarizing the muscle fiber membrane. The signal spreads along T-tubules to the sarcoplasmic reticulum, which releases calcium ions (Ca2+). Ca2+ binds troponin, causing tropomyosin to shift and expose myosin binding sites on actin. Myosin heads (energized by ATP hydrolysis) bind actin forming cross-bridges. The power stroke pulls actin toward the sarcomere center. ATP binds myosin, breaking the cross-bridge, and the cycle repeats as long as Ca2+ and ATP are available. When stimulation stops, Ca2+ is pumped back into the SR, tropomyosin re-covers binding sites, and the muscle relaxes.

rubric.key: Correct description of neuromuscular junction events
Accurate role of calcium and regulatory proteins
Complete cross-bridge cycle with ATP role

questions.question: A patient's X-ray report describes a 'comminuted fracture of the left proximal femur with displacement.' Break down each anatomical term and explain what this means clinically.
questions.answer: Comminuted: the bone is broken into three or more fragments. Left: left side of the body. Proximal: the end of the femur closest to the hip joint. Femur: the thigh bone, the longest and strongest bone in the body. Displacement: the bone fragments are not aligned in their normal anatomical position. Clinical meaning: this is a severe hip fracture, likely resulting from significant trauma (in younger patients) or a fall with osteoporosis (in elderly). It will likely require surgical reduction (ORIF - open reduction internal fixation) with plates, screws, or possibly hip replacement. This has significant implications: high risk of complications including DVT, fat embolism, and infection. The patient will need extensive rehabilitation.

questions.question: Compare the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions of the autonomic nervous system. Describe at least four organs and their response to each division.
questions.answer: Sympathetic (fight-or-flight) vs. Parasympathetic (rest-and-digest): (1) Heart: sympathetic increases rate and contractility; parasympathetic decreases rate. (2) Lungs: sympathetic dilates bronchioles for more air; parasympathetic constricts bronchioles. (3) Eyes: sympathetic dilates pupils (mydriasis); parasympathetic constricts pupils (miosis). (4) GI tract: sympathetic decreases motility and secretion; parasympathetic increases motility and secretion. (5) Blood vessels to skeletal muscle: sympathetic dilates for increased blood flow; parasympathetic has minimal effect. The sympathetic system prepares the body for emergencies while the parasympathetic promotes everyday maintenance functions.

rubric.key: At least 4 organs with both divisions described
Responses correctly paired to each division

questions.question: Explain the pathophysiology of Type 2 diabetes mellitus, including the roles of insulin resistance, beta cell dysfunction, and the progression from pre-diabetes to disease.
questions.answer: Type 2 diabetes develops progressively: (1) Insulin resistance: target cells (muscle, liver, fat) become less responsive to insulin, requiring higher insulin levels to achieve normal glucose uptake. Contributing factors include obesity (especially visceral fat), sedentary lifestyle, and genetics. (2) Compensatory hyperinsulinemia: pancreatic beta cells increase insulin production to overcome resistance, maintaining near-normal glucose levels. This is the pre-diabetic phase (fasting glucose 100-125 mg/dL or A1c 5.7-6.4%). (3) Beta cell failure: over years, the increased demand leads to progressive beta cell exhaustion and apoptosis. Insulin production becomes insufficient to overcome resistance. (4) Overt diabetes: fasting glucose exceeds 126 mg/dL or A1c exceeds 6.5%. Chronic hyperglycemia damages blood vessels (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy, cardiovascular disease) through oxidative stress and advanced glycation end products.

rubric.key: Pre-diabetes to diabetes progression with lab values

major_assignments.title: Clinical Anatomy Case Study Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Create a portfolio of five clinical case studies, each focusing on a different body system. For each case, describe the relevant anatomy and physiology, explain the pathophysiology of the condition, and identify the clinical data elements (labs, imaging, vitals) that would appear in the patient's health record.
objectives: Apply anatomical and physiological knowledge to clinical scenarios
Connect normal anatomy to pathological conditions
Identify clinical data elements associated with each body system
Develop clinical reasoning skills relevant to health informatics
deliverables: Five case study analyses (2-3 pages each)
Anatomical diagrams annotated for each case
Data element table for each case: labs, vitals, imaging findings, ICD-10 codes
Summary presentation of most interesting case (10 minutes)

criteria: Correct anatomical descriptions for each case
Connection from normal to abnormal physiology
Clinical presentation logically derived from pathophysiology
Relevant labs identified with normal and abnormal values
Vital sign abnormalities connected to physiology
Clear pathophysiology connecting normal and abnormal

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of anatomy and physiology concepts by body system

laboratory.description: Hands-on labs including dissection, histology, and physiology experiments

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of tissues, skeletal, muscular, and nervous systems

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all body systems with clinical application

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Missing more than 2 labs results in grade reduction. Lecture attendance strongly encouraged.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day. Lab reports due within one week. Practical exam dates are non-negotiable.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab work in pairs. All written assignments and exams individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Anatomy atlas images may be referenced but diagrams must be student-created or properly attributed.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Lab coat and safety goggles. Virtual anatomy software access provided. Internet access for research.
